1: wayyaʿan ʾîyôḇ wayyōʾmar: 'But Job answered and said.' Job's reply in ch. 21 is his most sustained and direct confrontation with the friends' retribution theology. It is also his most intellectually rigorous speech: he does not appeal to his own innocence (that was ch. 16-17, 19) but to observable reality — the empirical observation that the wicked frequently prosper and die peacefully. The chapter is a systematic dismantling of Zophar's thesis in ch. 20.
2: šimᵉʿû šāmôaʿ millatî wᵉhî tᵉhî tanḥûmōṯêḵem: 'Listen carefully to what I say; let this be your consolation.' Šāmôaʿ šāmaʿ (infinitive absolute + finite verb = listen carefully, truly listen) — the imperative doubled for emphasis. The sardonic offer: 'let my listening be your consolation' reverses the normal consolation formula. The friends came to console Job (2:11); he asks instead that they accept consolation by listening to his refutation of their theology. The consolation they owe him they will receive by being corrected.
3: śᵉʾûnî wᵉʾānōḵî ʾăḏabbēr wᵉʾaḥar ḏabbᵉrî talʿîg: 'Bear with me so I may speak; and after I have spoken, mock away.' Śāʾ (to lift, bear, endure) — 'endure me, carry me through this speech.' Job asks for the minimal courtesy of an uninterrupted hearing before the mockery begins. The offer to let them mock afterward has a brittle dignity: he does not expect understanding, only silence long enough to make the argument. The Hebrew word for 'mock' (lāʿag) is the same used of enemies mocking in the Psalms (Ps 22:7; 44:13).
4: hăʾānōḵî lᵉʾādām śîḥî wᵉʾim-maddûaʿ lōʾ-tiṣr rûḥî: 'As for me, is my complaint directed to man? Why then should I not be impatient?' Śîaḥ (complaint, meditation, musing) — Job distinguishes the addressee of his complaint: it is directed to God (ʾĕlôah), not to humans. If God is his interlocutor and God is silent, his impatience (qāṣar rûaḥ = short of breath/spirit, frustrated, impatient) is understandable. The distinction matters: his complaint is a theological dispute, not a social one, and therefore does not require the friends' approval or resolution.
5: pᵉnû ʾēlay wᵉhiššāmmû wᵉśîmû yaḏ ʿal-peh: 'Look at me and be appalled; lay your hand over your mouth.' Šāmam (to be appalled, devastated, deserted) — the same root used of the desolated temple (Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11) and waste land. Job's condition is so extreme that visual encounter with it should produce the physical gesture of stunned silence: śîm yaḏ ʿal-peh (lay the hand over the mouth) is the gesture of awed, speechless respect (Job 29:9; 40:4; Mic 7:16). Job is asking the friends to consider that what they are seeing should silence their comfortable theodicy.
6: wᵉʾim-zāḵarᵉtî wᵉniḇhāltî ûʾāḥaz bᵉśārî palāṣûṯ: 'Even when I think of it I am troubled; shuddering seizes my flesh.' Nāḇah (niphal = to be terrified, overwhelmed) and pālaṣ (shuddering, shivering with horror — a hapax). The physical response (shuddering flesh) to intellectual reflection on his question anticipates the divine speech from the whirlwind (chs. 38-41) where the appropriate human response to God's question-fronted reality is precisely this awe. Job is epistemically honest: the question he is about to ask is one that genuinely disturbs him, not a rhetorical device.
7: maddûaʿ rᵉšāʿîm yiḥyû ʿāṯᵉqû gam-gāḇᵉrû ḥāyil: 'Why do the wicked go on living, grow old, and even grow mighty in power?' The central theodicy question that runs through the Psalms of Asaph (Ps 73:3-12), Jeremiah's complaints (Jer 12:1-2), Habakkuk (Hab 1:2-4), and Ecclesiastes (Eccl 7:15; 8:14). Yiḥyû (they live, they go on living), ʿāṯaq (to grow old — the opposite of dying young), gāḇar ḥāyil (grow mighty in strength) — the triple accumulation describes not just survival but increasing prosperity. This directly contradicts Zophar's claim in 20:5 that 'the triumph of the wicked is short.'
8: zarʿām nāḵôn lip̄nêhem ʿimmām ûṣeʾĕṣāʾêhem lᵉʿênêhem: 'Their children are established around them, and their offspring before their eyes.' In the ANE theological framework, prosperous descendants were the primary sign of divine favor. The wicked man sees his children and grandchildren growing up in front of him — the most concrete embodiment of blessing and continuity. This too directly contradicts the standard retribution formula which should see the wicked man's family cut off (cf. Ps 37:28; Prov 14:11).
9: bāttêhem šālôm mippāḥad wᵉlōʾ šêḇeṭ ʾĕlôah ʿălêhem: 'Their houses are safe from fear; the rod of God is not upon them.' Šālôm mippāḥad (peace from fear) — the kind of security promised to the righteous (Prov 1:33; Ps 91:5) is actually enjoyed by the wicked. Šēḇeṭ ʾĕlôah (the rod/staff of God) — the instrument of discipline and judgment is conspicuously absent from the wicked man's household. The observation is theologically scandalous: the God who should be punishing them is apparently protecting them instead.
10: šôrô ʿibbār wᵉlōʾ yiḡʿāl pārāṯô pālᵉṭāh wᵉlōʾ tᵉšakkēl: 'Their bull breeds without fail; their cow calves without miscarriage.' Agricultural fertility and successful livestock reproduction were direct signs of covenant blessing (Deut 28:4; 28:11). The wicked man's animals breed reliably — the same blessing promised to obedient Israel is enjoyed by those who refuse God (v.14-15). The biological evidence of divine favor is distributed without apparent moral sorting.
11: yᵉšallᵉḥû kᵉṣōʾn ʿᵉwîlêhem wᵉyaldêhem yᵉraqᵉḏûn: 'Their little ones run out like a flock, and their children dance.' The festive domestic image of children and dancing is the biblical picture of a community at peace and flourishing (Jer 31:13; Zech 8:5; Ps 30:11). The wicked man's household is characterized by exactly the visible joy and abundance that his theology should have denied him. The dancing children are a pointed counter-image to the suffering Job's household (ch. 1-2) where real piety produced disaster.
12: yiśśᵉʾû ṯōp̄ wᵉḵinnôr wayyiśmᵉḥû lᵉqôl ʿûḡāḇ: 'They take up timbrel and harp and rejoice at the sound of the flute.' Three instruments: tōp̄ (timbrel/tambourine), kinnôr (lyre/harp), ʿûḡāḇ (a wind instrument, possibly pipes or flute). The same instruments associated with worship and celebration (Ps 150:3-5; 1 Sam 10:5) are here in the hands of those who reject God (v.14). The irony is deliberate: the wicked celebrate with the same music Israel uses for divine praise, and their celebration is not interrupted by divine judgment.
13: yᵉḵallû ḇaṭṭôḇ yᵉmêhem ûḇeregaʿ šᵉʾôl yēḥāṯû: 'They spend their days in prosperity and go down to Sheol peacefully (bᵉrageaʿ).' Bālāh (to wear out, finish off, exhaust) is used of clothes wearing out (Deut 8:4; 29:5; Josh 9:13) and of time being consumed; here their days are 'worn out' in prosperity — fully spent in good. Bᵉrageaʿ šᵉʾôl yēḥāṯû: 'in a moment they go down to Sheol' — a peaceful, sudden death in old age at the peak of prosperity, not the prolonged dying of the punished wicked that Zophar described. The contrast with Job's prolonged suffering is implicit and pointed.
14: wayyōʾmᵉrû lāʾēl sûr mimmennû wᵉḏaʿat dᵉrāḵeḵā lōʾ ḥāp̄āṣnû: 'Yet they say to God, “Leave us alone; we do not want to know your ways.”' Job quotes the wicked man's own words — their explicit rejection of God. Sûr mimmennû (depart from us) echoes Israel's wilderness rebellion and the prophetic accusation that the people have turned away from YHWH (Isa 30:11; Jer 2:31). The explicit rejection of divine instruction (ḏaʿat dᵉrāḵeḵā) while enjoying full material prosperity is the datum that destroys simple retribution theology: prosperity is not correlated with piety in observed experience.
15: mah-šaddāy kî naʿăḇᵉḏennû ûmah-nôʿîl kî nip̄gᵉʿ-bô: 'Who is the Almighty that we should serve him? What do we gain by praying to him?' The wicked man's explicit calculus: God is worth serving only if the service produces material benefit. Yāʿal (to profit, benefit, avail) — what is the return on investment of prayer and obedience? This is the same transactional theological framework the Adversary implied in 1:9-10 ('Does Job fear God for nothing?'). The wicked man's explicit utilitarian theology is the dark mirror of the Adversary's accusation against Job's piety.
16: hēn lōʾ ḇᵉyādām ṭûḇām ʿăṣat rᵉšāʿîm rāḥᵉqāh mimmennî: 'Indeed, their prosperity is not in their own hand — the counsel of the wicked is far from me.' Job makes an important qualification: the wicked man's prosperity is not self-generated but is within God's domain. This prevents a deistic conclusion (the wicked prosper because God is absent). Job is insisting that divine sovereignty extends to the wicked's prosperity — God allows it — while simultaneously distancing himself from the wicked man's theology (rāḥᵉqāh mimmennî = I keep far from me).
17: kammāh nēr-rᵉšāʿîm yiḏʿāḵ wᵉyāḇōʾ ʿălêhem ʾêḏām ḥᵉḇālîm bᵉʾappô yᵉḥallēq: 'How often is the lamp of the wicked actually extinguished? How often does disaster come upon them? God distributes pain in his anger.' Nēr rᵉšāʿîm (lamp of the wicked) — the friends promised this lamp would be put out (18:5-6), but Job asks: how often does this actually happen? The rhetorical question (kammāh = how many times?) implies: rarely, or less often than the friends claim. The empirical challenge: observable experience contradicts the confident assertion of retribution theology.
18: yihyû kᵉteḇen lip̄nê-rûaḥ ûkᵉmōṣ gānāḇathu sûp̄āh: 'They are as straw before the wind, and chaff swept away by the tempest.' Job does not deny that the wicked sometimes face judgment; his challenge is the frequency and consistency that the friends assert. The images of straw/wind and chaff/storm are the standard biblical metaphors for the wicked's fate (Ps 1:4; Isa 17:13; Hos 13:3). Job acknowledges these happen — but how often? The point is the gap between theological assertion and empirical frequency.
19: ʾĕlôah yiṣp̄ōn laḇānāyw ʾônô yᵉšallem ʾēlāyw wᵉyēḏāʿ: 'You say, “God stores up the father’s punishment for his children.” Let him repay the man himself and he will know it.' Job attacks a specific theodicy escape route: when the wicked man himself does not suffer, appeal is made to his children bearing the punishment (Exod 20:5; Deut 5:9). Job demands direct personal punishment: if God is just, the transgressor himself should know it, not merely his descendants. Yēḏāʿ (he will know/experience it) — the experiential knowledge that comes from direct encounter with consequences.
20: yirʾû ʿênāyw kîḏô wᵉmēḥămaṯ šaddāy yišteh: 'Let his own eyes see his destruction; let him drink of the wrath of the Almighty.' The demand for first-person moral experience: the transgressor's own eyes (ʿênāyw) must witness his judgment, not a surrogate's. Šāṯāh ḥămaṯ šaddāy (drink the wrath of the Almighty) — the cup of divine wrath is one of the OT's most vivid judgment images (Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17, 22; Jer 25:15-17; Rev 14:10; 16:19). Job insists that moral accountability requires direct personal experience, not generational displacement.
21: kî mah-ḥep̄ṣô bᵉḇêṯô ʾaḥărāyw ûmispar ḥodāšāyw ḥuṣṣāṣû: 'For what pleasure does he take in his house after him, when the number of his months is severed?' The forward-looking question: once the man himself is dead, what does he care about his house and posterity? The logic undermines the delayed-generational-punishment argument: if the wicked man is dead, he has no experiential stake in the fate of his children. Justice requires contemporaneous consequences, not posthumous ones. Ḥāṣaṣ (to cut, divide, sever) — his months are 'cut short' in death.
22: hăʾēl yᵉlammēḏ-dāʿat wᵉhûʾ rāmîm yišpôṭ: 'Will anyone teach God knowledge? He is the one who judges those on high.' A rhetorical boundary-setting: the question in v.7 (why do the wicked prosper?) does not license human instruction of God about how to run his moral universe. Rāmîm (the high ones, the exalted) — possibly angels or heavenly beings; God judges those above Job's level. The acknowledgment of God's sovereignty does not resolve Job's theodicy question but prevents the question from becoming an attempt to rewrite God's governance. The tension between epistemic humility and honest complaint is sustained rather than resolved.
23: zeh yāmûṯ bᵉʿeṣem tummô kullô šalʾănān wᵉšāleō: 'One person dies in full vigor (bᵉʿeṣem tummô), completely at ease and secure.' ʿOṣem (bone, strength, essence) + tōm (completeness, integrity, wholeness) — 'in the very bone of his completeness'; at the apex of his strength and life-fullness. Šalʾănān wᵉšāleō (at rest and at ease) — the state of prosperous security that Ps 73:12 describes as the wicked man's condition. The observation in vv.23-26 is deliberately universal: all kinds of people die, with no apparent moral correlation to their quality of death.
24: ʿăṭînāyw mālᵉʾû ḥālāḇ ûmaḥ ʿaṣᵉmôṯāyw yᵉšuqqeh: 'His sides full of milk and his bones saturated with marrow.' The physical description of peak-health death: ḥālāḇ (milk/fat — used figuratively for abundant nourishment) and maḥ (marrow — the innermost richness of the bones). Both are images of abundant interior nourishment — this man dies with every biological indicator of flourishing. The grotesque health at death makes the contrast with Job (whose bones burn with fever, 30:17; whose skin clings to his bones, 19:20) all the more pointed.
25: wᵉzeh yāmûṯ bᵉnep̄eš mārāh wᵉlōʾ-ʾāḵal baṭṭôḇāh: 'Another person dies in bitterness of soul, having never tasted what is good.' The exact opposite life-trajectory to v.23-24: nep̄eš mārāh (bitter of soul) — the phrase used of Hannah (1 Sam 1:10) and Naomi (Ruth 1:20: 'call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt bitterly with me'). This person has suffered throughout life and has 'not eaten of the good' — never experienced the prosperity that should, according to retribution theology, reward the righteous. The pairing of vv.23-26 presents four deaths that cut across the moral categories: prosperous death, bitter death — then...
26: yaḥḏāw ʿal-ʿāp̄ār yiškaḇû ûrimāh tᵉḵassēh ʿălêhem: 'They lie down together in the dust, and worms cover them both.' Yaḥḏāw (together) — the most theologically loaded word in the verse. The prosperous man of v.23-24 and the bitter man of v.25 lie together, undifferentiated in death. Rimmāh (worm, maggot) covers them equally — the same image of final corruption for all flesh (Isa 14:11; 66:24; Job 17:14). The leveling of death cuts through all moral sorting: the grave receives the righteous and wicked alike without apparent distinction, which is exactly the empirical observation that makes retribution theology inadequate as a complete account of divine justice.
27: hēn yāḏaʿtî maḥšᵉḇôṯêḵem ûmᵉzimmôṯ ʿālay taḥmōsû: 'I know your thoughts and the schemes you wrongfully contrive against me.' Job names the interpretive move the friends are about to make: maḥăšāḇôt (plans, schemes, inner thoughts) and mᵉzimmôt (devious plans, malicious intentions — from zāmam = to plan evil). The friends will argue that Job is the wicked man of their description; Job preempts this by naming it as a wrong (tāḥmōs = to do violence to, wrong) and demonstrating that he knows their rhetorical play before they execute it.
28: kî tōʾmᵉrû ʾayēh bêṯ-nāḏîḇ wᵉʾayēh ʾōhel miškᵉnôṯ rᵉšāʿîm: 'For you say, “Where is the house of the noble man? Where are the dwelling places of the wicked?”' The friends' implied argument: the prosperity of the wicked is always destroyed — look, their houses are gone, their tents ruined. The rhetorical question (ʾayēh = where is it?) implies the answer is 'destroyed,' validating retribution theology. Job will rebut this with a traveler's report (v.29-31): the evidence from the roads tells a different story.
29: hălōʾ šᵉʾaltum ʿôḇᵉrê ḏāreḵ wᵉʾōṯōṯām lōʾ tᵉnakkērû: 'Have you not asked those who travel the roads? Do you not recognize their evidence?' Job appeals to empirical testimony — travelers who have observed the world broadly, not just within the friends' theological tradition. ʿôḇᵉrê ḏāreḵ (those who pass by on the road) — witnesses who have seen many places and many lives. ʾōṯōṯām (their signs, testimonies) — the observable evidence they can report. Job's empirical method here is striking: he appeals to experience and observation against the friends' appeal to tradition (cf. Zophar's 'Do you not know from of old...?' 20:4).
30: kî leḥeḏ rāʿ yēḥāśēḵ bᵉyôm ʿeḇrôṯ yûḇālû: 'That the wicked is spared on the day of disaster and delivered to the day of wrath.' The traveler's report contradicts the friends: the wicked are often spared on the day of disaster — not judged, but protected. Yôm ʿeḇrôṯ (day of wrath) — possibly a future eschatological event rather than the personal death Zophar described. Job may be acknowledging a final judgment while insisting it does not reliably operate in present experience. The gap between present observation and final accountability is the unresolved theological space the book of Job inhabits.
31: mî-yaggîḏ ʿal-pānāyw darkô ûmah-pāʿal mî yᵉšallem-lô: 'Who will tell him to his face what he has done? Who will repay him for what he has done?' The double question highlights the gap between sin and observable consequence: no one confronts the powerful wicked man with his deeds while he lives, and no retributive agent appears to repay him during his lifetime. The silence of accusation and the absence of repayment during life is the empirical observation that Job offers against the friends' tidy theology of immediate retribution.
32: wᵉhûʾ liqᵉḇārôt yûḇāl wᵉʿal-gādîš yišqôḏ: 'Yet he is carried to the grave, and a vigil is kept over his tomb.' The wicked man receives the full burial honors due to the respected: šāqaḏ (to watch, keep vigil) over his gādîš (tomb-mound, burial heap). This is high-status burial — a watched and honored grave. The contrast between the wicked man's death without consequences and Job's own suffering is complete: the man who rejected God (v.14-15) receives public honor in death while Job, who maintains his integrity, is abandoned and suffers.
33: māṯqû-lô riḇᵉḏê nāḥal wᵉʾaḥărāyw kāl-ʾāḏām yimšôḵ wᵉlip̄ānāyw ʾên mispar: 'The clods of the valley are sweet to him, and every person follows after him, as countless as those who went before him.' Māṯaq (sweet, pleasant) — even the earth's embrace of the wicked is gentle. The numberless procession of humanity — those who came before and those who follow after — makes the wicked man's death part of the universal flow of mortality rather than a punitive end. He dies with the company of all humanity; his death is not a special judgment but a common destiny.
34: wᵉʾêḵ tᵉnaḥămûnî hāḇel ûtᵉšûḇôṯêḵem nišʾar māʿal: 'How then do you comfort me with empty words? Your answers are nothing but falsehood!' Heḇel (vanity, breath, emptiness) — the key Ecclesiastes word: the friends' consolation is vapor, insubstantial. Māʿal (treachery, faithlessness) — a strong term used of covenant betrayal (Lev 5:15; Ezra 9:4; 1 Chr 10:13). The friends' false answers constitute not mere error but a kind of faithlessness — a betrayal of their duty as truth-speakers and consolers. Job ends the speech with the same verdict YHWH will pronounce in 42:7: 'you have not spoken of me what is right.'
